description: Knowledge captured from a dialogue on whether incentives can be scaled back without eroding cooperation. Measured/sourced findings are tagged 'measured value'; the conversation's own conjectures are tagged 'assumed value' so the most load-bearing assumptions can later be surfaced for scrutiny.

words.name: incentive
words.description: something that motivates or encourages an agent to act in a particular way.

words.name: trust
words.description: the willingness of one party to become vulnerable to another party on the presumption that the other will act in a beneficial manner.

words.name: cooperation
words.description: the process of groups of organisms working or acting together for common, mutual, or some underlying benefit.

words.name: motivation
words.description: the reason or reasons one has for acting or behaving in a particular way.

words.name: intrinsic
words.description: behaviour driven by internal rewards, arising from within the individual.

words.name: crowding
words.description: in motivation theory, the effect by which an external intervention undermines (crowds out) or strengthens (crowds in) intrinsic motivation.

words.name: fine
words.description: a monetary penalty imposed as punishment for an offence.

words.name: price
words.description: the quantity of payment given by one party to another in return for goods or services.

words.name: commons
words.description: the cultural and natural resources accessible to all members of a society, including natural materials such as air, water, and a habitable earth.

words.name: common-pool
words.description: a resource whose size or characteristics make it costly to exclude potential beneficiaries from obtaining benefits from its use.

words.name: resource
words.description: a source or supply from which a benefit is produced.

words.name: reciprocity
words.description: in social psychology, a social norm of responding to a positive action with another positive action.

words.name: institution
words.description: a humanly devised structure of rules and norms that shape and constrain social behaviour.

words.name: seasonality
words.description: the presence of variations that occur at specific regular intervals less than a year, such as seasonal weather.

words.name: latitude
words.description: a geographic coordinate that specifies the north-south position of a point on the surface of a body.

words.name: confounder
words.description: a variable that influences both the dependent variable and independent variable, causing a spurious association.

words.name: enlightenment
words.description: an intellectual and philosophical movement that dominated Europe in the 17th and 18th centuries, with global influences.

words.name: autonomy
words.description: the capacity of an agent to act in accordance with self-given principles; in Kant, the ground of moral obligation.

words.name: hysteresis
words.description: the dependence of the state of a system on its history; a path-dependent persistence of an effect after its cause is removed.

words.name: Cuba
words.description: a country comprising the island of Cuba in the Caribbean.

words.name: casa-particular
words.description: private family lodging offered to travellers in Cuba, legalised as a form of private enterprise.

words.name: Norway
words.description: a Nordic country in Northern Europe.

words.name: Nauru
words.description: an island country in Micronesia, in the southwestern Pacific Ocean.

words.name: Russia
words.description: a country spanning Eastern Europe and North Asia.

words.name: transition
words.description: the process of change from a centrally planned to a market economy.

words.name: open-source
words.description: software released under a licence in which the copyright holder grants the rights to use, study, change, and distribute the software and its source code.

words.name: measured value
words.description: qualifier marking a value as empirically measured / sourced from a study rather than assumed.

words.name: assumed value
words.description: qualifier marking a value as an assumption or conjecture to be verified later, not an established measurement.

triples.name: intrinsic motivation
triples.description: motivation arising from within the individual rather than from external reward.

triples.name: crowding effect
triples.description: the effect by which an external incentive displaces intrinsic motivation.

triples.name: incentive can crowd out intrinsic motivation
triples.description: the claim that introducing an external incentive can reduce (crowd out) pre-existing intrinsic motivation.

triples.name: fine is a price
triples.description: the interpretation that a monetary penalty reframes a moral obligation as a purchasable service, so it may increase rather than deter the penalised behaviour.

triples.name: commons governance
triples.description: self-organised management of a common-pool resource by its users.

triples.name: commons governance without market or state
triples.description: the finding that user communities can sustainably govern common-pool resources through reciprocity and graduated sanctions, without privatisation or central control.

triples.name: resource curse
triples.description: the phenomenon that countries with an abundance of non-renewable natural resources tend to have less economic growth and worse development outcomes than countries with fewer resources.

triples.name: sovereign wealth fund
triples.description: a state-owned investment fund that invests in real and financial assets, often funded by commodity revenue.

triples.name: resource curse conditional on weak institutions
triples.description: the thesis that resource wealth harms growth only where institutions are weak (grabber-friendly), and helps where institutions are strong (producer-friendly).

triples.name: time preference
triples.description: the current relative valuation placed on receiving a good at an earlier date compared with receiving it at a later date.

triples.name: agricultural origin of time preference
triples.description: the thesis that pre-industrial agro-climatic conditions favouring higher return to agricultural investment generated a persistent long-term orientation.

triples.name: seasonality causes long-term orientation
triples.description: CONJECTURE: that the need to plan half a year ahead under marked seasons teaches longer-term planning. Held in the dialogue as weakly supported.

triples.name: latitude as confounder
triples.description: the methodological caveat that latitude correlates with seasonality, disease burden, soils and early state formation alike, so a seasonality-geduld correlation may be spurious.

triples.name: low-trust equilibrium
triples.description: a self-sustaining social state in which low generalised trust makes cooperation costly, which keeps trust low.

triples.name: trust erosion hysteresis
triples.description: CONJECTURE: that once trust erodes, restoring the prior incentive does not restore cooperation, because the return path differs from the outbound path.

triples.name: incentive reduction can raise trust
triples.description: the claim (supported by crowding-out evidence) that reducing external incentives can increase intrinsic motivation and trust rather than reduce cooperation.

triples.name: fast transition can destroy intermediary structures
triples.description: the diagnosis that the failure of post-Soviet liberalisation was driven by the speed and sequence of reform destroying intermediary trust networks, not by liberalisation as such.

triples.name: Cuba casa particular
triples.description: the rapid emergence of a private lodging sector after legalisation, cited as recovery from SUPPRESSED (not eroded) cooperation, since family trust networks stayed intact.

triples.name: Nauru phosphate
triples.description: phosphate mining that funded a rent-based economy; when the resource was exhausted, a revivable cooperation/work structure was lacking.

triples.name: Norway sovereign wealth fund
triples.description: the systematically built fund cited as the exception requiring explanation: oil met an already-consolidated rule-of-law state.

triples.name: open-source cooperation
triples.description: large-scale valuable work sustained without a price incentive, carried by meaning, reputation and reciprocity.

triples.name: scaling problem of commons governance
triples.from: commons governance without market or state
triples.description: OPEN QUESTION: whether group-sanction-based cooperation that works in small units scales to anonymous mass societies; transparency proposed as a substitute for mutual acquaintance.

sources.name: Gneezy Rustichini 2000
sources.description: Gneezy U, Rustichini A (2000). A Fine Is a Price. Journal of Legal Studies 29(1):1-17. Field experiment: a fine for late daycare pickup increased lateness and the effect persisted after removal.

sources.name: Titmuss 1970
sources.description: Titmuss RM (1970). The Gift Relationship: From Human Blood to Social Policy. London: Allen & Unwin. Argues paying for blood donation can reduce voluntary supply and quality.

sources.name: Ostrom 1990
sources.description: Ostrom E (1990). Governing the Commons: The Evolution of Institutions for Collective Action. Cambridge University Press. Documents long-lived user-governed common-pool resource regimes, e.g. Toerbel (Wallis) Alpine commons.

sources.name: Galor Oezak 2016
sources.description: Galor O, Oezak O (2016). The Agricultural Origins of Time Preference. American Economic Review 106(10):3064-3103. Pre-industrial agro-climatic return to investment predicts contemporary long-term orientation.

sources.name: Mehlum Moene Torvik 2006
sources.description: Mehlum H, Moene K, Torvik R (2006). Institutions and the Resource Curse. The Economic Journal 116(508):1-20. The resource curse operates through the quality of institutions.

values.note: Boolean: empirically observed that the fine increased the penalised behaviour (crowding out). 1 = effect observed.

words: incentive can crowd out intrinsic motivation
values.note: Crowding-out of intrinsic motivation by monetary incentive; supported by Titmuss (blood) and Gneezy/Rustichini (fines).

words: commons governance without market or state
values.note: User-governed commons sustained for centuries (Toerbel/Wallis from the 13th c.) without market or state.
Pre-industrial agro-climatic return to investment predicts present long-term orientation; selection/adaptation/learning over long horizons.

words: resource curse conditional on weak institutions
values.note: Resource curse mediated by institutional quality (grabber- vs producer-friendly).

CONJECTURE of the dialogue. ~0.4 = weak-to-moderate subjective confidence. Partially backed by Galor/Oezak but confounded by latitude and contradicted by the Russia counter-case (harsh seasons, no fund-mentality). Candidate for closer testing.
Methodological caveat: latitude co-varies with almost everything, so a seasonality-patience correlation is likely partly spurious. ~0.7 subjective confidence that confounding is material.

CONJECTURE: eroded (not merely suppressed) trust does not recover by restoring the prior incentive. Cuba (suppressed -> fast recovery) does NOT confirm it; Russia/1990s and Nauru are the candidate confirming cases. Distinguishing measure to watch: state of intermediary trust networks, not 'apathy yes/no'. High-leverage assumption to verify.

Stronger than a bare conjecture because crowding-out evidence (Titmuss, Gneezy/Rustichini) backs the mechanism; but its scaling to society-wide incentive reduction is untested. ~0.6 subjective confidence.

words: fast transition can destroy intermediary structures
values.note: Diagnosis that speed/sequence, not liberalisation per se, drove the post-Soviet failure; firsthand and 'Red Notice' as qualitative testimony. Supports gradualism. ~0.65 subjective confidence.

OPEN: whether small-unit reciprocity scales to anonymous mass societies. Transparency-as-substitute-for-acquaintance is the proposed but unproven bridge — the locus where the zukunft.com infrastructure would act.
